does: This paper proves a fundamental impossibility result in AI oversight: when a principal uses a strictly proper scoring rule to elicit truthful reports from an agent who also has non-accuracy incentives (approval for action, resource allocation, control), the principal's optimal screening mechanism necessarily induces miscalibration. The core contribution is demonstrating that any non-affine approval function required for optimal screening undermines truthful reporting when deviations are undetectable. The authors provide a closed-form formula characterizing this inevitable perturbation and prove the impossibility holds universally across all strictly proper scoring rules. They then construct an escape: a step-function approval threshold achieves first-best screening while preserving calibration by exploiting the binary structure of the agent's inflate-or-not choice, offering a practical resolution to the theoretical impossibility.
connects: This work extends classical mechanism design and principal-agent theory to the AI oversight setting, responding to growing interest in scalable oversight of autonomous agents. It builds on the framework of strictly proper scoring rules for eliciting predictions, which have deep roots in decision theory and forecasting. The paper connects to recent work on AI alignment and oversight that recognizes agents face multiple objectives beyond accuracy, and to broader mechanism-design literature studying how to screen agent types through contract design. The endogeneity problem—where the mechanism itself changes incentives—mirrors classical issues in institutional design, but the paper's contribution is showing how this endogeneity specifically breaks calibration in the oversight context, unifying insights from scoring theory with principal-agent problems.
enables: This result opens several directions for AI oversight: first, it justifies the practical appeal of step-function thresholds in approval systems, providing theoretical grounding for binary decision mechanisms. Second, it suggests limitations of purely accuracy-focused oversight when agents have real-world incentives, pointing toward hybrid approaches that explicitly account for multiple agent objectives. Third, the impossibility framework could inform research into alternative oversight structures—perhaps relaxing strict propriety, using information-revelation mechanisms, or designing institutional checks that don't rely solely on scoring rules. The work also raises questions about the fundamental limits of scalable oversight and whether calibration can be maintained under competing incentives, potentially motivating investigation into completely different oversight paradigms for autonomous systems.
